hero_summary: Basandida is a relatively uncommon composite maqam whose karar is Rast (C), whose qawi is Nawa (G), and whose sayr is descending-ascending. In practical Arabic explanation it may be taught as Jins Nawa Athar on Rast (C), followed by Jins Rast on Nawa (G), with a half-cadence on Nawa colored by Busalik before returning to Rast.
short_identity: A composite maqam that settles on Rast (C) but is strongly centered around Nawa (G), with an upper formation related to Busalik/Nishabur before returning to the tonic.

quick_facts.common_start: It usually begins around Nawa (G), where the half-cadence appears in a Busalik color, before eventually returning to Rast (C)

sayr.summary: Basandida is a relatively uncommon composite maqam, and classical Turkish references attribute its construction to Sultan Selim III. Its karar is Rast (C), and its sayr is descending-ascending. In practical Arabic explanation it can be approximated as a maqam built from Jins Nawa Athar on Rast (C), then Jins Rast on Nawa (G), before returning to the tonic Rast. This explanation is pedagogically useful, though it simplifies a broader theoretical structure that links the maqam to Nishabur/Busalik material in the upper region together with Rast or Ajamli Rast at the tonic.
resting_tones: Rast (C): the final tonic of the maqam
Nawa (G): the qawi/ghammaz and the point of the half-cadence in a Busalik color
The final complete cadence is achieved by returning clearly to Rast (C)
motion_notes: The phrase usually begins around Nawa (G), because it is the qawi and the place of the half-cadence
The upper-middle region carries material close to Busalik/Nishabur before the maqam returns to the tonic
Its identity is not understood from the scale alone, but from this path: centering on Nawa, then returning to Rast
It should not be performed as if it were plain Rast or plain Nawa Athar
common_path: Highlight the central upper-middle area around Nawa (G)
Establish the half-cadence there in a Busalik color
Move through the maqam’s composite internal structure
Return to Rast (C) with a complete cadence

common_modulations: Basandida settles on Rast (C) like Rast itself, but it does not begin from the simple logic of basic Rast; its important center is Nawa (G), and it carries material close to Busalik/Nishabur before returning to the tonic

In practical Arabic explanation it can be approximated as beginning with Jins Nawa Athar, but the difference is that Basandida settles in the upper region on Rast on Nawa, whereas Nawa Athar asserts itself more strongly through Hijaz on Nawa together with Nikriz at the tonic

The theoretical reference links Basandida with a Nishabur-like construction around Busalik, but the maqam does not resolve there; it returns to Rast and establishes its own identity through that composite path
There is no real disagreement about its tonic and qawi, but there is a difference between the practical teaching summary and the fuller Turkish theoretical explanation

examples.note: The reference explicitly lists clear examples by Sultan Selim III in this maqam
